subject: mathematics
ncdc_competency: The learner applies algebraic and geometric techniques to analyse spatial relationships, loci, and conic sections applicable in real-world contexts.

lessons.title: Loci — Equations of Curves from Geometric Conditions

content.body: A locus is the set of all points satisfying a given geometric condition. Finding the equation of a locus means letting P=(x,y) be a general point, writing the condition algebraically, and simplifying.

Procedure:
1. Let P(x,y) be a general point on the locus.
2. Write the geometric condition using distance, gradient formulae.
3. Simplify to Cartesian equation.

Key distance formula: PA = √((x−a)²+(y−b)²)
Distance from P(x,y) to line ax+by+c=0: |ax+by+c|/√(a²+b²)

content.title: Example 1: Perpendicular bisector as locus
content.body:
Find locus of P equidistant from A(1,3) and B(5,1).
PA=PB: (x−1)²+(y−3)²=(x−5)²+(y−1)²
Expand: −2x−6y+10=−10x−2y+26
8x−4y=16 → 2x−y=4
This is the perpendicular bisector of AB.

content.title: Example 2: Apollonius circle
content.body:
Find locus of P with PA/PB=2, A=(0,0), B=(6,0).
PA=2PB: x²+y²=4[(x−6)²+y²]
x²+y²=4x²−48x+144+4y²
3x²+3y²−48x+144=0
x²+y²−16x+48=0 → (x−8)²+y²=16
Apollonius circle: centre (8,0), radius 4.

content.title: Example 3: Angle condition
content.body:
Find locus of P with ∠APB=90°, A=(−4,0), B=(4,0).
PA⊥PB: (y/(x+4))(y/(x−4))=−1
y²=−(x²−16)=16−x²
x²+y²=16
Circle of radius 4 (Thales' theorem — AB is diameter).

GPS triangulation: Distance conditions to multiple satellites give spheres whose intersection locates a receiver. Uganda's NaLIS land information system uses GPS locus mathematics for precise boundary surveys.

questions.question: Find the locus of P equidistant from A(0,0) and the line y=−4.
questions.explanation: Distance from A: √(x²+y²). Distance from y=−4: |y+4|. Equal: x²+y²=(y+4)². x²=8y+16=8(y+2).

questions.question: P equidistant from A(2,0) and B(−2,0). Locus:
Locus of P always 5 units from origin:
From (3,15) and (7,35), find x when y=20.
Locus of P: ∠OPA=90° where O=(0,0), A=(4,0). The equation is:
PA/PB=k≠1 always gives:
questions.explanation: The locus PA/PB=k (constant, k≠1) is always an Apollonius circle. Only k=1 gives a straight line (perpendicular bisector).

questions.question: P equidistant from A(0,4) and B(0,−4). Locus:
The gradient method uses two data points to:
questions.explanation: The gradient method connects two known data points with a straight line and uses that line's gradient to estimate unknown values — assuming the function is approximately linear between the points.

lessons.title: The Parabola — Definition, Equation, Tangent, and Normal

content.body: The parabola is the locus of a point equidistant from a fixed point (focus) and a fixed line (directrix). It models projectile paths, satellite dish reflectors, and suspension bridge cables.

Standard parabola y²=4ax:
Focus: F=(a,0)
Directrix: x=−a
Vertex: (0,0)
Axis: y=0
Parametric point: (at²,2at)

Tangent at (at²,2at): ty=x+at²
Normal at (at²,2at): y+tx=2at+at³

At point (x₁,y₁) on y²=4ax:
Tangent: yy₁=2a(x+x₁)

Focal chord parameter product: t₁t₂=−1

content.title: Example 1: Elements of y²=12x
content.body:
4a=12→a=3. Focus (3,0), directrix x=−3, vertex (0,0).
Point (3t²,6t) is general parametric point.
At t=1: P=(3,6). Tangent: y=x+3. Normal: y+x=9.

content.title: Example 2: Tangent and normal at (2,4) on y²=8x
content.body:
4a=8→a=2. P=(2,4). Parametrically: at²=2,2at=4→t=1.
Tangent: 1·y=x+2·1²=x+2 → y=x+2
Normal: y+1·x=2(2)(1)+2(1)³=4+2=6 → x+y=6

content.title: Example 3: Condition for tangency
content.body:
y=mx+c tangent to y²=4ax when c=a/m.
Proof: substitute y=mx+c: (mx+c)²=4ax.
m²x²+(2mc−4a)x+c²=0. Discriminant=0:
(2mc−4a)²=4m²c² → 4m²c²−16mac+16a²=4m²c²
−16mac+16a²=0 → c=a/m ✓

Satellite dishes: Uganda's Mpoma earth station uses parabolic dishes. The focus-directrix property ensures equal path lengths from incoming parallel waves to the focal receiver, giving maximum signal concentration at the focus.

questions.question: Normal to y²=8x at (2,4):
questions.explanation: Normal: y+tx=2at+at³. t=1,a=2: y+x=4+2=6. All three options express the same line.

questions.question: Gradient of tangent to y²=4ax at (at²,2at):
questions.explanation: Tangent ty=x+at² has gradient 1/t. Also dy/dx=2a/y=2a/(2at)=1/t. Both equivalent.

questions.question: For a focal chord with parameter t₁ at one end, the other end has parameter:
questions.explanation: Focal chord property: t₁t₂=−1, so t₂=−1/t₁.

lessons.title: The Ellipse — Equation, Properties, and Tangents

content.body: The ellipse is the locus of P where PF₁+PF₂=2a (constant sum of distances to two foci). It models planetary orbits, architecture, and optical instruments.

Standard ellipse x²/a²+y²/b²=1, a>b>0:
Major axis 2a (along x), minor axis 2b (along y)
Foci (±c,0) where c²=a²−b²
Eccentricity e=c/a<1
Parametric: (a cosθ, b sinθ)

Tangent at (x₁,y₁): xx₁/a²+yy₁/b²=1
Normal at (x₁,y₁): a²x/x₁−b²y/y₁=a²−b²

Tangency condition for y=mx+c:
c²=a²m²+b²

content.title: Example 2: From foci and sum
content.body:
Foci (0,±3), sum=10→a=5 (y-axis). c=3→b²=25−9=16→b=4.
Equation: x²/16+y²/25=1.

content.title: Example 3: Earth's orbit
content.body: a=1.496×10⁸ km, e=0.0167. c=ae≈2.5×10⁶ km. Perihelion=a−c≈1.47×10⁸km (Jan). Aphelion=a+c≈1.52×10⁸km (Jul). Near-circular orbit (e≈0) keeps temperatures stable.
Architecture: Elliptical arches in Uganda's Parliament building and classical structures use (acosθ,bsinθ) parametric form for AutoCAD specifications.

questions.question: When is x²/a²+y²/b²=1 a circle?
questions.explanation: When a=b: x²/a²+y²/a²=1 → x²+y²=a². A circle.

questions.question: 9x²+4y²=36 in standard form and eccentricity:
questions.explanation: Divide by 36: x²/4+y²/9=1. a²=9>b²=4 (major axis y). c²=9−4=5. e=√5/3.

lessons.title: The Circle — Advanced Properties and Radical Axis

content.body: The general circle equation, power of a point, and radical axis extend S5 circle work to advanced concepts used in network planning and computational geometry.

General circle: x²+y²+2gx+2fy+c=0
Centre (−g,−f), radius r=√(g²+f²−c)

Power of P(x₁,y₁): x₁²+y₁²+2gx₁+2fy₁+c
Positive→outside; zero→on circle; negative→inside
Tangent length from P (outside): √(power)

Radical axis of S₁=0 and S₂=0: S₁−S₂=0
Perpendicular to line joining centres.
If circles intersect: radical axis = common chord.

content.title: Example 2: Radical axis
content.body:
S₁: x²+y²−4x+2y−5=0
S₂: x²+y²+6x−4y−3=0
S₁−S₂: −10x+6y−2=0 → 5x−3y+1=0
Perpendicularity check: gradient of S₁−S₂ is 5/3; line of centres has gradient (2−(−1))/(−3−2)=−3/5. Product=−1 ✓

content.title: Example 3: Tangent length
content.body:
Tangent from P(5,7) to x²+y²−2x−4y−20=0.
Power=25+49−10−28−20=16. Tangent=√16=4.
Mobile network handover: MTN/Airtel signal coverage circles share a radical axis (equal signal strength boundary). Engineers use S₁−S₂=0 to find optimal handover boundaries.

questions.question: Power of P(3,4) w.r.t. x²+y²=25:
questions.explanation: Power=9+16−25=0. P is on the circle.

questions.question: Radical axis of x²+y²=4 and x²+y²−4x+2y=0:
g²+f²−c<0 means:
questions.explanation: r²=g²+f²−c<0 → no real radius → empty set.

questions.question: The radical axis is perpendicular to:
questions.explanation: Key property: radical axis ⊥ line connecting circle centres.

questions.question: Two circles intersect at A and B. The radical axis:
options: Is perpendicular to AB at midpoint
questions.explanation: Both intersection points have power=0 for both circles, so both lie on the radical axis.

content.body: The hyperbola is the locus of P where |PF₁−PF₂|=2a (constant difference of distances to foci). It models LORAN navigation, cooling tower shapes, and the graph of xy=k (Boyle's law).

Standard hyperbola x²/a²−y²/b²=1:
Vertices (±a,0), foci (±c,0), c²=a²+b²
Eccentricity e=c/a>1
Asymptotes y=±(b/a)x

Rectangular hyperbola xy=c²:
Parametric: (ct,c/t)
Tangent at (ct,c/t): x+t²y=2ct
Normal: y−c/t=t²(x−ct)
Asymptotes: x=0 and y=0 (coordinate axes)

content.title: Example 3: Asymptotes
content.body:
As x→∞ on x²/a²−y²/b²=1: y→±(b/a)x.
Combined asymptote equation: x²/a²−y²/b²=0.
Hyperbola approaches but never crosses asymptotes.
LORAN navigation: Ships measure time difference Δt between two radio signals → constant distance difference → hyperbola. Position found at intersection of two hyperbolas from different station pairs.

questions.question: Eccentricity of a hyperbola is always:
Tangent to xy=c² at (ct,c/t):
questions.explanation: Differentiating xy=c²: y+xy'=0 → y'=−y/x=−1/t². Tangent: y−c/t=(−1/t²)(x−ct) → t²y−ct=−x+ct → x+t²y=2ct.

questions.question: Ellipse: PF₁+PF₂=2a. Hyperbola: |PF₁−PF₂|=:
questions.explanation: For a hyperbola, the constant difference is 2a (the transverse axis length).

questions.question: Combined asymptote equation of x²/a²−y²/b²=1:
questions.explanation: Asymptotes satisfy y=±(b/a)x i.e. b²x²=a²y² i.e. x²/a²−y²/b²=0.

questions.question: Boyle's law PV=k is a:
questions.explanation: PV=k has the form xy=k — a rectangular hyperbola with asymptotes along the axes.

content.body: All conics share a unified focus-directrix definition with eccentricity e. The discriminant Δ=B²−4AC identifies the conic type from the general second-degree equation.

Unified definition: PS/PM=e
e<1: ellipse; e=1: parabola; e>1: hyperbola

General conic: Ax²+Bxy+Cy²+Dx+Ey+F=0
Discriminant Δ=B²−4AC:
Δ<0: ellipse (circle if B=0,A=C)
Δ=0: parabola
Δ>0: hyperbola

Chord of contact from P(x₁,y₁):
• Circle x²+y²=r²: xx₁+yy₁=r²
• Ellipse x²/a²+y²/b²=1: xx₁/a²+yy₁/b²=1
• Parabola y²=4ax: yy₁=2a(x+x₁)

Tangency condition y=mx+c to x²/a²+y²/b²=1: c²=a²m²+b²

content.title: Example 1: Classify conics by discriminant
content.body:
a) 4x²+9y²=36: A=4,B=0,C=9. Δ=−144<0 → ELLIPSE
b) y=x²+2x: A=1,B=0,C=0. Δ=0 → PARABOLA
c) x²−y²=1: A=1,B=0,C=−1. Δ=4>0 → HYPERBOLA
d) x²+xy+y²=3: A=1,B=1,C=1. Δ=1−4=−3<0 → ELLIPSE (rotated)

content.title: Example 2: Tangency condition
content.body:
Find c for y=2x+c to be tangent to x²/4+y²/3=1.
c²=a²m²+b²=4(4)+3=19. c=±√19.

content.title: Example 3: Line tangent to parabola
content.body:
y=2x+k tangent to y²=16x.
Method 1: discriminant of substitution=0.
(2x+k)²=16x → 4x²+(4k−16)x+k²=0.
Discriminant: (4k−16)²−16k²=0 → −128k+256=0 → k=2.
Method 2: tangency condition c=a/m. a=4,m=2. c=4/2=2. k=2 ✓

Cassegrain telescope: Makerere University physics observatory uses parabolic primary mirror + hyperbolic secondary. The combined conic geometry concentrates parallel starlight to a compact focal point — direct application of conic properties.

questions.question: Condition for y=mx+c tangent to y²=4ax:
questions.explanation: Substituting and setting discriminant=0 gives c=a/m.

questions.question: Chord of contact from P(2,3) to x²+y²=25:
y=3x+c tangent to x²/4+y²/1=1. Find c.
Eccentricity of a parabola:
questions.explanation: By definition, e=1 for a parabola.

questions.question: Eccentricity order for ellipse, parabola, hyperbola:
Chord of contact from P(x₁,y₁) to y²=4ax:
questions.explanation: Chord of contact from external point to y²=4ax: yy₁=2a(x+x₁).

key_concepts: Locus: let P=(x,y), write geometric condition, simplify. Equal distances→perpendicular bisector; ratio condition→Apollonius circle; right angle at P→circle on chord as diameter.
Parabola y²=4ax: focus (a,0), directrix x=−a. Tangent at (at²,2at): ty=x+at². Tangency y=mx+c: c=a/m.
Ellipse x²/a²+y²/b²=1 (a>b): c²=a²−b², e<1, foci (±c,0). Tangent at (x₁,y₁): xx₁/a²+yy₁/b²=1. Condition: c²=a²m²+b².
Hyperbola x²/a²−y²/b²=1: c²=a²+b², e>1, asymptotes y=±(b/a)x. Rectangular hyperbola xy=c²: tangent x+t²y=2ct.
Circle x²+y²+2gx+2fy+c=0: centre (−g,−f), r=√(g²+f²−c). Power of P: x₁²+y₁²+2gx₁+2fy₁+c.
Radical axis S₁−S₂=0: perpendicular to line of centres; = common chord when circles intersect.
Discriminant Δ=B²−4AC: Δ<0 ellipse, Δ=0 parabola, Δ>0 hyperbola.
Chord of contact: circle xx₁+yy₁=r²; ellipse xx₁/a²+yy₁/b²=1; parabola yy₁=2a(x+x₁).
common_errors: Ellipse: c²=a²−b² (minus). Hyperbola: c²=a²+b² (plus). Confusing these is the most common error.
Power is positive outside, zero on, negative inside circle.

questions.question: Find locus of P equidistant from A(3,0) and B(−3,0).
y=3x+c tangent to x²/4+y²/1=1:
questions.explanation: c²=4(9)+1=37. c=±√37. Options A and B are equivalent.

questions.question: Radical axis of x²+y²=4 and x²+y²−4x=0:
Tangent to xy=4 at (2,2):
questions.explanation: c=2,t=1. Tangent x+t²y=2ct: x+y=4. Same as 2x+2y=8 (×2).

questions.question: Eccentricities: parabola, ellipse, hyperbola in order:
Two ellipse foci at (0,±4) and sum PF₁+PF₂=10. Equation:
A planet with e≈0.01 has an orbit that is:
questions.explanation: e close to 0 means the foci are very close to the centre, making the ellipse nearly circular. For Earth e=0.0167 — its orbit is nearly a circle.
